pdf_candidates.text_excerpt: Chemosphere 82 (2011) 1380–1386 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect Chemosphere journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/chemosphere Quantitative determination of ﬂuorochemicals in municipal landﬁll leachates Carin A. Huset a,1, Morton A. Barlaz b, Douglas F. Barofsky a,c, Jennifer A. Field c,d,⇑ a Department of Chemistry, Oregon State University, Corvallis, OR 97331, United States b Department of Civil, Construction and Environmental Engineering, North Carolina State University, Raleigh, NC 27695, United States c Environmental Health Sciences Center, Oregon State University, Corvallis, OR 97331, United States d Department of Environmental and Molecular Toxicology, Oregon State University, Corvallis, OR 97331, United States a r t i c l e i n f o a b s t r a c t Article history: Twenty-four ﬂuorochemicals were quantiﬁed in landﬁll leachates recovered from municipal refuse using Received 11 June 2010 an analytical method based on solid-phase extraction, dispersive-carbon sorbent cleanup, and liquid Received in revised form 5 November 2010 chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry. The method was applied to six landﬁll leachates from four Accepted 25 November 2010 locations in the US as well as to a leachate generated by a laboratory bioreactor containing residential Available online 30 December 2010 refuse. All seven leachates had the common characteristic that short-chain (C4–C7) carboxylates or sulfo- nates were greater in abundance than their respective longer-chain homologs (PC8). Perﬂuoroalkyl car- Keywords: boxylates were the most abundant (67 ± 4% on a nanomolar (nM) basis) ﬂuorochemicals measured in Fluorochemicals Perﬂuorochemicals leachates; concentrations of individual carboxylates reaching levels up to 2800 ng L 1. Perﬂuoroalkyl sul- Landﬁll leachate fonates were the next most abundant class (22 ± 2%) on a nM basis; their abundances in each of the seven PFOA leachates derived from municipal refuse were greater for the shorter-chain homologs (C4 and C6) com- PFBA pared to longer-chain homologs (C8 and C10). Perﬂuorobutane sulfonate concentrations were as high as PFOS 2300 ng L 1. Sulfonamide derivatives composed 8 ± 2.1% (nM basis) of the ﬂuorochemicals in landﬁll leachates with methyl (C4 and C8) and ethyl (C8) sulfonamide acetic acids being the most abundant. Flu- orotelomer sulfonates (6:2 and 8:2) composed 2.4 ± 1.3% (nM basis) of the ﬂuorochemicals detected and were present in all leachates. Ó 2010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction In 2007, 54% of municipal solid waste was disposed of in landﬁlls in the US (US Environmental Protection Agency, 2007). Municipal One of the primary applications of ﬂuorochemicals is to coat so- sewage sludge, which contains ﬂuorochemicals (Higgins et al., lid materials such as paper and packaging (including food wrap- 2005), is also landﬁlled (US Environmental Protection Agency, pers), textiles, and carpets (3M, 2001; Kissa, 2001). The use of ﬂuorochemicals Landﬁlls are anaerobic ecosystems (Christensen et al., 2001), and is now documented for food packaging and cookware (Sinclair leachate is the term given to water that percolates through the re- et al., 2007; Begley et al., 2008), paper (Stadalius et al., 2006), tex- fuse. Leachates are highly concentrated with organic contaminants, tiles (Washburn et al., 2005), and carpeting (Washburn et al., salts, and dissolved organic matter (Christensen et al., 2001; 2005).
